examples.title: Dijkstra's Shortest Path Algorithm
examples.description: Finds shortest path from a source vertex to all other vertices in a weighted graph with non-negative weights
examples.code:
DIJKSTRA(Graph G, Vertex source)
BEGIN
  FOR each vertex v IN G.V
    d[v] ← ∞          // Initialize distances
    parent[v] ← NIL   // For path reconstruction
  d[source] ← 0
  
  PriorityQueue Q ← all vertices
  
  WHILE Q ≠ ∅ DO
    u ← EXTRACT-MIN(Q)   // Vertex with minimum distance
    
    FOR each neighbor v OF u
      IF d[u] + weight(u,v) < d[v] THEN
        d[v] ← d[u] + weight(u,v)
        parent[v] ← u
        UPDATE-QUEUE(Q, v)   // Update priority queue
      END IF
    END FOR
  END WHILE
  
  RETURN d, parent
END
examples.explanation: The algorithm starts with source distance 0 and all others as infinity. It repeatedly extracts the vertex with minimum distance and relaxes edges to neighbors, updating shorter paths when found. Uses a priority queue for efficiency.

examples.title: Bellman-Ford Algorithm
examples.description: Finds shortest paths from source to all vertices, handles negative edge weights
examples.explanation: Bellman-Ford relaxes all edges V-1 times to find shortest paths. If any edge can still be relaxed in the Vth iteration, a negative weight cycle exists. Unlike Dijkstra, it handles negative weights but has higher time complexity.

examples.title: Kruskal's Minimum Spanning Tree
examples.description: Greedy algorithm to find MST by sorting edges and using Union-Find data structure
examples.code:
KRUSKAL-MST(Graph G)
BEGIN
  // Sort all edges by weight in non-decreasing order
  SORT edges by weight
  
  FOR each vertex v IN G.V
    MAKE-SET(v)    // Create disjoint sets
  
  MST ← ∅
  
  FOR each edge (u,v) IN sorted_edges
    IF FIND-SET(u) ≠ FIND-SET(v) THEN
      MST ← MST ∪ {(u,v)}
      UNION(u, v)   // Merge sets
    END IF
  
  RETURN MST
END

// Union-Find operations
MAKE-SET(x)
  parent[x] ← x
  rank[x] ← 0

FIND-SET(x)
  IF parent[x] ≠ x THEN
    parent[x] ← FIND-SET(parent[x])  // Path compression
  RETURN parent[x]

UNION(x, y)
  IF rank[x] > rank[y] THEN
    parent[y] ← x
  ELSE
    parent[x] ← y
    IF rank[x] = rank[y] THEN
      rank[y] ← rank[y] + 1
examples.explanation: Kruskal's algorithm sorts all edges and adds them one by one if they don't form a cycle. The Union-Find data structure efficiently manages disjoint sets to detect cycles. It's a greedy algorithm that produces optimal MST.
examples.timeComplexity: O(E log E) or O(E log V)

examples.title: BFS for Graph Traversal
examples.description: Breadth-First Search to explore graph level by level, useful for finding shortest path in unweighted graphs
examples.explanation: BFS explores vertices level by level using a queue. It starts from a source, marks it visited, adds neighbors to queue, and processes them in FIFO order. Guarantees shortest path in unweighted graphs.
examples.timeComplexity: O(V²) with adjacency matrix, O(V+E) with adjacency list
